@garethmj  Why do you expect this test to fail? You set the scope to session via cmd_line and the derive a fixture form chrome_options fixture which should be session scoped? 

I would expect test cases like this:

cmdline flag set (seee example): 
* session scoped fixture passes
* function scoped fixture fails
cmdline flag not set:

```
        """
        import pytest
        @pytest.mark.fixture(scope="session")
        def my_fixture(<selenium_fixture_where_scope_can_be_determined>):
            return

       # define this in testfile
       def test_stuff(my_fixture):
           assert True
    """
    testdir.quick_qa("--selenium-session-scope", passed=1)

...
        """
        import pytest
        @pytest.mark.fixture(scope="function")
        def my_fixture(<selenium_fixture_where_scope_can_be_determined>):
            return

       # define this in testfile

    """

I am fine a single `otLinkedBuffer` in "send-done" as well. 

To me the key point is that we document the behaviro in API description. Specially that, user can call `otTcpActiveSendByReference` again after a previous successful call and before "send done" callback. And how/when the "send done" callback will be invoked.

There may other behavior to consider and describe:
- In general, since the current model on tx path is that we expect the `otLinkedBuffers` to be initially created and owned by the app layer (OT API user), we need to think of how to notify it so it can reclaim its buffers in all failure cases.
- For example, what if the socket gets disconnected? Do we invoke "send done" with some failure for each still  remaining `otLinkedBufffer` (to give chance to app layer to reclaim its buffers?) Or a new callback?

I'm wondering here if it would be terrible for the _grpc_stub_interpreter.py for this `error_message` definition to just call grpc-device's `GetErrorMessage`. The inputs and outputs are the same, just how it calculates the error message size is different and an implementation detail. You can see nimi-python's metadata [here](https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/blob/master/src/niscope/metadata/functions.py#L3169) and grpc-device's [here for GetErrorMessage](https://github.com/ni/grpc-device/blob/main/source/codegen/metadata/niscope/functions.py#L2400)

So, I guess I could have a custom template or something that gets invoked for this NI-SCOPE to generate the call to grpc-device's `GetErrorMessage`. I guess I could also look at overriding the metadata so `grpc_name' is just 'GetErrorMessage' and 'included_in_proto' evaluates to true.

Either way, does that sound reasonable? Otherwise, I guess I'd look at exposing `GetErrorMessage` for nimi-python and using that instead of `error_message` but that seems like a more daunting change.

Ok, yeah - this caught a bug. When I eliminated `iter_platform_args()`, that killed sending Pip the `--platform ... --implementation ... --python-version ... --abi ...` quad. The foreign_platform patches cover `--platform ... --implementation ... --abi ...`, aka: wheel tag matching, but they do not cover `--python-version ...`, aka `Requires-Python` matching. Pip requires you send the quad as a whole; so the `--python-version ...` / `Requires-Python` matching must be patched too. That patching is already implemented in the locker patches; so I'll need to share that code here for foreign platform resolves.

Objection: my code can be written by anyone who knows python, your code can be written by anyone who knows internal implementation of channels in PyMeasure.

Which method is more natural and obvious for people not familiar with internal implementation?

> > data structures with indexing to handle the case we want to support.

> The use case for this is not indexing, though, but iteration! Indexed access is handled by `instr.prefix_id` specifically because it's more concise than having to do `instr.channels["id"]`. Also, this offers autocomplete of matching entries in the REPL, which a dictionary does not.
> 
Here, I was referring to the general case of channels who are, in my view, iteration of same set of functions with an index to distinguish them.

My point is that you have invented a new method of indexing for the following reasons:
- ch_A is shorter than channels['A'] (obviously why `ch` in one case and `channels` in the other for comparison is interesting)
- REPL does offer autocompletion for dictionary indexes

> > this potential duplication costs nothing to us

what I meant is if I create a figure object like:

```
>>> n = notation.Figure(1, '#', extender=True)
```

then the `.isExtender` property can get out of date:

```
>>> n.isExtender
True
>>> n.hasExtender = False
>>> n.isExtender
True
```

instead don't store `isExtender` anywhere and recalculate it on the fly.  Let's rename it
to `pureExtender` or `isPureExtender` since that makes it clear what the difference with
hasExtender is:

```
@property
def pureExtender(self):
    '''
    Read-only boolean property that returns True if an extender is part of the 
    figure but no number is given (a number of 1 means no-number). It is a pure extender.

    >>> n = notation.Figure(1, '#', extender=True)
    >>> n.isExtender
    True
    >>> n.number = 2
    >>> n.isExtender
    False
    '''
    return self.number == 1 and self.hasExtender
```
and then it will always be up-to-date no matter what.

_Conclusion: I think what's in the PR is the best solution because it's easier to understand and the timings are so close anyways that I'm having a tough time getting any definitive timings._

I tested a couple different combinations, including the original (`base`) and found that on my laptop with 10 billion iterations, handling the exclusive-or case in a way that made sense took _way_ more time than I thought it would, doubling the function's apparent runtime.

It's worth noting that any improvements in times are only in the hundredsths or thousandths of a second faster over 10 billion runs, so averaged over 10 billion runs we're talking about differences of trillionths of a second per function call.  This is a level of timing precision that is unreliable, so I'm getting conflicting results over repeated runs.

So, that being said, the usually fastest version of `isclose` that I've found on my laptop a tossup between what's in the PR and a somewhat more convoluted implementation that sometimes reduces the number of times `isnan` is called:
```pyrex
cdef int _is_close(double x, double y, double abs_delta, double rel_delta):
    if isnan(x) and isnan(y):
        return 1
    return abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))

cdef int _is_close_xor_switch(double x, double y, double abs_delta, double rel_delta):
    if isnan(x):
        return isnan(y)
    return abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))
```

Here are one example of timing results:
```
2.0 elapsed time (base):  4.7931s 1
2.0 elapsed time (pr):    4.693s 1
2.0 elapsed time (xor1):  8.8043s 1
2.0 elapsed time (xorf):  8.7695s 1
2.0 elapsed time (xors):  4.6966s 1
2.0 elapsed time (xor):   8.7564s 1
nan elapsed time (base):  4.6996s 0
nan elapsed time (pr):    4.6963s 0
nan elapsed time (xor1):  7.9259s 0
nan elapsed time (xorf):  7.9238s 0
nan elapsed time (xors):  4.6906s 0
nan elapsed time (xor):   7.9262s 0
```

Here's the source code:
```pyrex
cdef int _is_close_base(double x, double y, double abs_delta, double rel_delta):
    return abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))

cdef int _is_close(double x, double y, double abs_delta, double rel_delta):
    if isnan(x) and isnan(y):
        return 1
    return abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))

cdef int _is_close_xor(double x, double y, double abs_delta, double rel_delta):
    if isnan(x) and isnan(y):
        return 1
    if isnan(x) ^ isnan(y):
        return 0
    return abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))

cdef int _is_close_xor_once(double x, double y, double abs_delta, double rel_delta):
    cdef int x_is_nan = isnan(x)
    cdef int y_is_nan = isnan(y)
    if x_is_nan and y_is_nan:
        return 1
    if x_is_nan ^ y_is_nan:
        return 0
    return abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))

cdef int _is_close_xor_first(double x, double y, double abs_delta, double rel_delta):
    cdef int x_is_nan = isnan(x)
    cdef int y_is_nan = isnan(y)
    if x_is_nan ^ y_is_nan:
        return 0
    if x_is_nan & y_is_nan:
        return 1
    return abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))

cdef int _is_close_xor_switch(double x, double y, double abs_delta, double rel_delta):
    if isnan(x):
        return isnan(y)
    else:
        return abs(x-y) <= (abs_delta+rel_delta*abs(y))

# re-run this once I have an internet connection.
def test_isclose():
    cdef unsigned long n = 10000000000  # 10 billion iterations
    cdef unsigned long i
    cdef unsigned long sum_ = 0
    cdef double y_value
    for y_value in [2, float('nan')]:
        start_time = time.time()
        sum_ = 0
        for i in range(n):
            sum_ += _is_close_base(i, y_value, 1e-8, 1e-5)
        print(f'{y_value} elapsed time (base):  {round(time.time() - start_time, 4)}s {sum_}')

        start_time = time.time()
        sum_ = 0
        for i in range(n):
            sum_ += _is_close(i, y_value, 1e-8, 1e-5)
        print(f'{y_value} elapsed time (pr):    {round(time.time() - start_time, 4)}s {sum_}')

        start_time = time.time()
        sum_ = 0
        for i in range(n):
            sum_ += _is_close_xor_once(i, y_value, 1e-8, 1e-5)
        print(f'{y_value} elapsed time (xor1):  {round(time.time() - start_time, 4)}s {sum_}')

        start_time = time.time()
        sum_ = 0
        for i in range(n):
            sum_ += _is_close_xor_first(i, y_value, 1e-8, 1e-5)
        print(f'{y_value} elapsed time (xorf):  {round(time.time() - start_time, 4)}s {sum_}')

        start_time = time.time()
        sum_ = 0
        for i in range(n):
            sum_ += _is_close_xor_switch(i, y_value, 1e-8, 1e-5)
        print(f'{y_value} elapsed time (xors):  {round(time.time() - start_time, 4)}s {sum_}')

        start_time = time.time()
        sum_ = 0
        for i in range(n):
            sum_ += _is_close_xor(i, y_value, 1e-8, 1e-5)
        print(f'{y_value} elapsed time (xor):   {round(time.time() - start_time, 4)}s {sum_}')

```

This was a bit tough to navigate but this is the current flow:

1. Assume the first argument to a module's `__init__()`, if there is one, is a web3 instance
2. If it is, we pass it in and the module may make use of the instance in case it needs to make internal calls
3. If there is no argument to be accepted in the `__init__()`, it throws a type error and we catch it here and, instead, initialize the module without any arguments. This is a module that makes no use of the web3 instance.

I had some trouble with trying to read the first argument on the class because the class isn't initialized yet at this point. We are passing in a static class and these lines are actually where they get initialized. I think a separate (and maybe better approach?) is to create a utility method that reads the static class, more specifically the `__init__()` of the static class, and sees if there is an argument passed in. If there is, we can initialize it with `module_class(w3)`, if there is not then we initialize it as `module_class()`.

100% open to changing this if you see a better way. Thoughts?

It is usually an antipattern to test for None objects treating them as booleans:
https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#programming-recommendations

In this case, for example, if by chance the `unix_poll()` function would be given a large string, you would end up multiplying it by a factor of 1000 most likely sending the program to OOM.

It is always recommended to test explicitly against `None` values.

```python
timeout = timeout * 1000 if timeout is not None else None
```

Or more simply in this case:
```python
if timeout is not None:
    timeout *= MILLISECONDS

...

MILLISECONDS = 1000
```

With at least dependencies, every time we "pip install luigi" in a container image build, we risk getting a transitive rogue dependency. Given that Luigi typically runs with access to a data lake, such a scenario is a potential disaster.

That being said, it seems that the cure is worse than the disease in absence of a shading mechanism, and I agree that we should settle for at least dependencies. In JVM land, exact dependencies is the norm, but I guess it does not work out as well in Python. I guess that each build that uses Luigi has to take responsibility for the chain instead, e.g. by freezing transitive dependencies.

In our internal codebase, return OK for removing a non-existent entry is necessary because we need to make sure the operation is idempotent. GCS operations must be idempotent because we deal with redis reconnection and failed commands may be resent after redis failover.

However, return OK for removing non-existent entry can be buggy because the caller may try to remove an entry with slightly different field values. (it's usual in current codebase because we have a special field to mark its write mode, so we often only fill key fields such as ids if we want to append an entry with deletion mode) This results in unexpected behavior and hard to find the bug.

I'll change `RAY.SET_REMOVE` to return an error if the entry doesn't exist. And keep `RAY.SET_ADD` to return OK if the entry already exists. Sounds OK?

No it will not change. The executability in Windows is not defined by the mode, but by the file extension. Here is a demonstration (to be run on Windows of course):
```
#!/usr/bin/env python
import os
import subprocess
import tempfile

CONTENT = '''\
echo Hello World!
'''

with tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as path:
    test1 = os.path.join(path, 'test1.bat')
    test2 = os.path.join(path, 'test2.txt')

    with open(test1, 'w') as file_h:
        file_h.write(CONTENT)

    with open(test2, 'w') as file_h:
        file_h.write(CONTENT)

    print('Executable mode for test1 is: {0}'.format(os.access(test1, os.X_OK)))
    print('Executable mode for test2 is: {0}'.format(os.access(test2, os.X_OK)))

    try:
        subprocess.check_call([test1])
        print('test1 is effectively executable!')
    except OSError:
        print('test1 not so executable finally...')

    try:
        subprocess.check_call([test2])
        print('test2 is effectively executable!')
    except OSError:
        print('test2 not so executable finally...')
```

So there is not implementation planed for now to fix this functionality on Windows. The idea would be to check the file extension in this case. Would like to create an issue to track this down?

Actually, there's something else nasty lurking here. `csv` will _quote_ specific field values, and will also escape embedded quotes in the field data. That will result in expansion of the field values we are sending. For some `csv.writer` `w`, 

    w.writerow(['test stuff', 'fo"o'])

yields

    "test\nstuff","fo""o"\r\n

I don't immediately see a way around this other than either walking across all the record values and applying the expected CSV transformations, which is a bit nasty, or serializing the entire CSV file to disk before uploading it, which is quite nasty and a major change to the architecture. OK so, to sum up:

Say we're running Pants on machine P1, and executing processes remotely on machines M1, M2.

1. `Foo.proto` is a local source file, so it's already in the CAS and P1 has its fingerprint.

2. P1 calls `ExecuteProcess(protoc, Fingerprint(Foo.proto))`, sending the request to M1.

3.  M1 fetches `Foo.proto` from the CAS, runs `protoc` on it (I'm eliding the question of where M1 gets a `protoc` binary...) thus generating `Foo.scala`. 

4. M1 puts `Foo.scala` in the CAS, returning just `Fingerprint(Foo.scala)` to P1.

5. P1 calls `ExecuteProcess(scalac, Fingerprint(Foo.scala))`, sending the request to M2.

6. M2 fetches `Foo.scala` from the CAS, runs `scalac` on it, thus generating `Foo.class`.

7. M2 puts `Foo.class` in the CAS, returning just `Fingerprint(Foo.class)` to P1.

So P1 does not have the content of `Foo.scala` or `Foo.class` locally at any point. 

Does that about sum it up reasonably?

If so, one more question: what does that mean for running `ensure_remote_has_recursive` before step 5 above? P1 doesn't have `Foo.scala`'s content, so it seems like it can't ensure anything. It can I guess error out if the CAS is missing the file, but then so can M2 directly.

Adding `nogil` to function declarations just tells Cython that those functions **can** be used without the GIL, but it won't automatically release the GIL when they're called.

That's what you're doing with

```python
cpdef void h3_to_parent_vect(
    const H3Index[:] h,
    int res,
    H3Index[:] out
) nogil:
```
and when you're adding `nogil` to `h3ToParent` in `h3lib.pxd`; just saying these functions **can** be used without having the GIL.

The `with nogil` block is different. It is actually telling Cython to release and re-acquire the GIL before and after that block. That's what your error is about above: you're releasing the GIL, but as far as Cython knows (before you added `nogil`), `h3ToParent` requires the GIL.

I'm not sure stress test handle with the function tells us anything interesting. How about we just use the actor but varies the number of `hello_world` replicas? So the output tells us something like this:
```
With 1 forward replicas, 1 worker replicas, 1000 queries: 5.2s
With 1 forward replicas, 5 worker replicas, 1000 queries: 1.2s
With 1 forward replicas, 10 worker replicas, 1000 queries: 1.2s
Just calling the worker replicas, 1 worker replicas: 1000 queries: 3.2s
Just calling the worker replicas, 10 worker replicas: 1000 queries: 1.2s
```
*Note*: can we make the ForwardActor async?

What do we see here? 
- By comparing the forward replicas and just calling the worker replicas, we measure the (latency) overhead in handle. This answers the question: How much latency/throughput hit is there when I compose models?
- By comparing the total completion time as we fix the number of forward replica and varies the number of worker replicas, we measure the limit of a single async actor. This answers the question: How many "ForwardActor" or this kind of high level pipeline workers do I need to provision for my workload? every 1k qps, 2k qps?

Why remove pyupgrade?

Adding `torch` as a requirement here does only somewhat solve this:

1. `pip` will create a separate virtual env for these requirements, which means it will pull `torch` from PyPI. That means you will get a CPU version for macOS and Windows and an outdated CUDA version (10.2 IIRC) for Linux.
2. We only have the stable binaries up on PyPI, but in general we depend on the current nightly.

Hi @RunOrVeith - I've fixed the bare except. In Python 3, the entire stack trace is presented to the user when raising an exception inside the `except` block. By using `from e`, the error message is changed to say `The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception`. Reading the output, it will make it sound like `dataclass_json` caused the `encoder` function to have an exception, which is incorrect.

To make this read properly (since `dataclass_json` raise an exception that was directly caused by the `encoder`), we would need to do `raise e from ValueError()`. The problem this has is that it puts the explanation that helps the user at the top of the stack trace, and forces someone to scroll up and scan the trace.

Personally, I prefer the way it currently is as it both displays the entire trace including previous exceptions, but also places the most important piece at the bottom of the error output, without confusing language. Your thoughts on this?

API Spec:
```
def grad_global_norm(parameters, max_norm=None):
    """Calculate the 2-norm of gradients of parameters, and how much they should be scaled down
    such that their 2-norm does not exceed `max_norm`, if `max_norm` if provided.

    If gradients exist for more than one context for a parameter, user needs to explicitly call
    ``trainer.allreduce_grads`` so that the gradients are summed first before calculating
    the 2-norm.

    .. note::
        This function is only for use when `update_on_kvstore` is set to False in trainer.

    Example::

        trainer = Trainer(net.collect_params(), update_on_kvstore=False, ...)
        for x, y in mx.gluon.utils.split_and_load(X, [mx.gpu(0), mx.gpu(1)]):
            with mx.autograd.record():
                y = net(x)
                loss = loss_fn(y, label)
            loss.backward()
        trainer.allreduce_grads()
        norm = grad_global_norm(net.collect_params().values())
        ...

    Parameters
    ----------
    parameters : list of Parameters
    max_norm: NDArray, optional
        The maximum L2 norm threshold. If provided, `ratio` and `is_finite` will be returned.

    Returns
    -------
    NDArray
      Total norm. Shape is (1,)
    NDArray
      Ratio for rescaling gradients based on max_norm s.t. grad = grad / ratio.
      If total norm is NaN, ratio will be NaN, too.
      Returned if `max_norm` is provided. Shape is (1,)
    NDArray
      Whether the total norm is finite, returned if `max_norm` is provided. Shape is (1,)
    """

```

So the code here suppose to get the `chunk_end` value, becouse we want to stop reading the VCF as soon as we get through the target region. To do this, in my opinion, we have 3 solution:

1. The best solution: parse and check the record, once we get through the target region, stop reading. But the parse and check process has implement in the fellow process, so that is duplicate. PASS.

2. The easy understanding solution: using the linear index, read until the block which target region end position fall into. But this solution is a bit annoying to me. Becouse the linear index only track the begin offset, to get the end offset, we might need to parse the next chromosome index and/or check wether the block fully contain the record overlap our target region. PASS.

3. The solution here the code using: using the binning index, the binning index track both begin and end offset, so sort and get the biggest offset which is the point we stop reading the VCF.

ah -- this is the only substantive issue I see and disagree with -- I think it should be an error to have an ornament where the sum of the realized durations doesn't equal the length of the original.  In the case where there's an explicit delay float set, and an explicit quarterLength for the turn, the last note should be held to the length of the remaining unused duration.

This is common where there's a dotted-eighth + 16th pattern with a delayed turn on the eighth, where the turn is usually executed as 3 notes on the 2nd 16th and the last 16th holds the original note:

C[8.] D[16]  =>  C[16] D[32trip] C[32trip] B[32trip] C[16] D[16]

(which would make a great demo).  It's seen all over the music of Weber.

What I don't want happening is that there's a score with lots of ornaments and someone calls "realizeOrnaments" and now there's gaps in the stream.  That should be an error.

Quoting from https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/RTCRtpTransceiver/setCodecPreferences :

"Any codecs not included in codecs will not be considered during the process of negotiating a connection. This lets you prevent the use of codecs you don't wish to use."

.. so no, we are not setting a preference here, we are saying "it's this codec or nothing". This is a good thing too, because the codec is not negotiable if you're playing pre-encoded data. Here are the cases in which there would be a difference:
1) 
```python
AmqpAnnotatedMessage(...) # no bodies passed in

- Before: results `ValueError` b/c less than 1 body passed in.
- Now: created Message with value_body=None, without ValueError

AmqpAnnotatedMessage(sequence_body=..., value_body=...) # two bodies passed in, one is value_body

- Before: results in ValueError since >1 bodies passed in

My brain is a bit scrambled after reading about dates and times in Python so I'd double check everything I'm saying here, but I _think_ we want to use `utcfromtimestamp` here.

The datetime objects being used both in the variable `now` and in `next_update` regardless of the OCSP backend used are [naive rather than aware](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#aware-and-naive-objects). Maybe we should fix this, but I _think_ always using naive objects is fine as long as we're sure all of the datetime objects are in the same timezone.

I also _think_ [datetime.fromtimestamp](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp) converts the timestamp into local time while [datetime.utcfromtimestamp](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp) keeps it as UTC.

I checked this by setting a breakpoint and comparing the calculation done here to one done on a site like https://www.timeanddate.com/ and using `utfcfromtimestamp` at least matches the total number of seconds there.

Also, do you know what Apache's behavior is if we get this wrong and the time in the next update field passes before the TTL we give Apache? Depending on that and how confident we are this code is right, we may want to give ourselves more than an hour of leeway to prevent Apache from serving bad OCSP responses in case something goes wrong.

                self._global_cache = {}
```

I think we want to replace the cache regardless of if it's currently empty. 

1. Request A comes in. It tries to fetch an experiment that doesn't exist. This triggers reading the experiments config into memory but doesn't put anything in the cache.
2. Request A blocks on some IO like a database call and yields to the event loop.
2. Request B comes in. It tries to do some bucketing and finds the experiment config has changed. It re-reads from disk but because the global cache is currently empty it doesn't make a new cache object. Both request A and B are now pointing at the same cache despite having different config data. 
3. Request B now blocks on IO and yields.
4. Request A wakes back up and tries to do some more bucketing. This time it buckets for an experiment that _does_ exist and it places the data it calculates into the global cache. 
5. Request A finishes up.
6. Request B wakes back up and tries to use that same experiment. It's in the cache! But oh no, it's based on the old file. We've poisoned the cache with stale data.

Replacing the cache unconditionally is probably safest.

💭 We could probably deprecate and remove this functionality; I don't think anybody uses the command registries.

Short version is, I wanted to make it so that developers didn't have to add methods to the `Iota`/`StrictIota`/`MultisigIota`/etc. classes explicitly.  Instead, the registry would automatically load commands in the specified packages and attach them magically.

In exchange for being clever, this approach had a number of drawbacks:
* Very difficult to understand which commands a particular API class can use via static analysis.
* Difficult to document how users should invoke each command, what parameters to provide, etc.
* IDEs weren't couldn't support jumping from a command invocation to the corresponding class (e.g., `self.getNodeInfo()` \[via `__getattr__`\] vs. `core.GetNodeInfoCommand(self.adapter)()`).

In #30 I laid the groundwork for making command registries redundant.

There is an issue with the GCE metadata server where the ID Token returned does not include `email` or `email_verified` fields in the ID Token claims.

This is an issue because IAP rejects any ID tokens without an `email` claim. So any requests from GCE to an IAP-protected resource will fail.

The workaround is to include the query parameter `format=full` in the identity endpoint request. I don't like using `format=full` because it returns a LOT of information in the ID token. Also, we expect to have this issue fixed in the coming months, and if we remove `format=full`, that will be a BC breaking change. But this is probably better than releasing a broken integration.

This looks good. It should be possible to use `unsigned long long` and `uint64_t` interchangeably. Although the size of `long long` is machine-dependent, it will compile correctly on 64-bit machines and raise compilation errors on 32-bit machines, which is just as well, because we weren't expecting to compile this on such machines (it's better for errors to be explicit than implicit). Types in C are synonyms—it should be possible to use them interchangeably. If this doesn't work, it may be because `long long` is actually 128-bit, but I doubt it.

It's worth keeping in mind that the `atomicCAS` and the `atomicMin` are separate atomic operations: another thread could slip between them. But looking at the logic of this, that would be okay.

You might want to abstract this out into a `__device__` function that updates a `struct Error`. It will be more difficult getting the error string in: the error condition is indicated by swapping a `char *` from `nullptr` to a statically defined string. Kernels that can report more than one error string could be tricky. Maybe you need an `error_flag` for each string?

The two atomic operations could be simplified into one by initializing `error_i` to one greater than the maximum possible `thread_id` (which is always known before launching a kernel: it's the length of the array you're parallelizing over) and only doing the `atomicMin`, no `atomicCAS`. Then if the `error_i` still has its initial value after the kernel is done, there were no errors. Simplifying this to one step could make dealing with multiple error strings less complicated. Maybe then you would just have an `error_i` for each string, and checking for an error at the end would be to find the string with the minimum number (after the kernel is done), and it's no error if it's in its initialized value.

```python
@nb.jit
def emd_1d(w_x, w_y, x, y):
    """EMD 1D Specialized to square euclidean distance with manual forward mode gradient"""
    n = w_x.shape[0]
    m = w_y.shape[0]
    
    idx_x = np.argsort(x)
    idx_y = np.argsort(y)
    
    sorted_x = x[idx_x]
    sorted_y = y[idx_y]
    
    sorted_w_x = w_x[idx_x]
    sorted_w_y = w_y[idx_y]
    
    cost = 0.
    grad = np.zeros(n)

    i = j = 0
    w_i = sorted_w_x[0]
    w_j = sorted_w_y[0]
    
    m_ij = 0.
    while True:
        diff = sorted_x[i] - sorted_y[j]
        m_ij = (diff) ** 2
        
        if w_i < w_j or j == m - 1:
            cost += m_ij * w_i
            grad[idx_x[i]] += 2 * w_i * diff
            i += 1
            if i == n:
                break
            w_j -= w_i
            w_i = sorted_w_x[i]
        else:
            cost += m_ij * w_j
            grad[idx_x[i]] += 2 * w_j * diff
            j += 1
            if j == m:
                break
            w_i -= w_j
            w_j = sorted_w_y[j]

    return cost, grad

```

I am not sure if it is possible to fix in ha-textfield. 

ha-textfield extends mwc-textfield, where `value` is a `string` property. 

The issue is that when user types `"-0"`, we get a event with value `"-0"` from mwc-textfield and convert it to Number, which is `-0`.

Then when we next render the ha-textfield, we set value to `-0`. But since value is a string, the number is implicitly converted to string. Unfortunately `Number(-0).toString()` is `"0"`, not `"-0"`. 

Without changing ha-textfield to accept number types, it never sees the `-0` input. 

I'm not sure if there is such a thing as a "more generic" fix, since I believe 0/-0 are the only numbers that have this issue.

- loop over just `msgs`
- use a `dict` accumulator mapping error `msg_id` to the count of that message type "seen so far" in the loop. Note that you can initialize the accumulator using all of the unique keys (see above comprehension) with initial corresponding value 0
- then inside the loop use the accumulator to decide whether to append a message to `output_lst` or not
- after that loop, use a second loop over `output_lst` that sets the `number_of_occurrences` for each output message---we don't need to bother with updating this for messages that aren't included in the output!

> encode returns bytes , why bytes is needed ?


The 'B' command returns 5 bytes which are not ment to be read as an ASCII encoding.
The Intstrument.control()/messurement()/setting() functions however do not offer a passing of binary values like Instrument.binary_read() does, this is why I used the encode call.
For reference I attach a exerpt of the manual 
![3478A_manual_pg_3-14](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17066917/116834062-6d107900-abf7-11eb-8358-2f00b69a2777.png)

> In a wider review context, I would like to propose to access the status bits returned by command "B" in a more structured way. In this mode, your code is probably slower, but much more readable, in my opinion.
My proposal is to define a static method which decodes the bytes returned by "B" command through the use of ctypes and bitfield (see attached examples test.zip)

I like your idea of a static method to decode the bits from the status bytes.
Speed is not an issue here, as the status of the instrument is normally not queried very quickly.
And btw this instrument is anyway not the fastest (being from the early '80ties)
Where speed was concerned was the measure routine, which now uses caching of the command to be sent.

According to Pypa,  `twine` is now the [official way of publishing python packages to Pypi](https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/packaging-projects/).  The `twine upload` command handles both registering the package name and uploading the distributions.

I typically have a Makefile in my python projects with the following commands:
```
## remove all build, test, coverage and Python artifacts
clean: clean-build clean-pyc clean-test

## remove build artifacts
clean-build:
	rm -fr build/
	rm -fr dist/
	rm -fr .eggs/
	find . -name '*.egg' -exec rm -f {} +

## remove Python file artifacts
clean-pyc:
	find . -name '*.pyc' -exec rm -f {} +
	find . -name '*.pyo' -exec rm -f {} +
	find . -name '*~' -exec rm -f {} +
	find . -name '__pycache__' -exec rm -fr {} +
	rm -rf .mypy_cache/

## remove test and coverage artifacts
clean-test:
	rm -fr .tox/
	rm -f .coverage
	rm -fr htmlcov/
	rm -fr .cache/
	rm -fr .pytest_cache

## builds source and wheel package
dist: clean
	python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel


## upload wheel and source distribution
upload: dist
	twine upload -r pypi dist/hexpy*

## builds source and wheel package
dist: clean
	python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel

```

With a `.pypirc` file in my home directory i can run `make upload` which will remove all previous artifacts, make a fresh build in the `dist/` directory and upload them to Pypi.

`twine upload -s` will also let you sign your distribution with GPG.

https://oss.redislabs.com/redisgraph/result_structure/

In short: result_set is a list `[column_names, data, metadata]`

This is a bug here -- one letter repository names like https://github.com/jayvdb/z will break moban. https://github.com/moremoban/moban/issues/299

moban must either

- first check if the syntax is *valid* as a library name or as a remote-repo name, *before* looking for a local resource.

Or

- first check if the resource is a local path (abs or relative) *and* check if that local name is shadowing an online resource, *and* tell the user that the online resource is being shadowed, otherwise they are going to be surprised.

Either approach is terribly inefficient!  But it is consistent.  And moban can tell the user to add `../` or `./` prefixes which are invalid library names and invalid remote-repo name, so that allows moban to quickly choose a local resource.

On reading and re-reading, this bit is not being handled in a strictly correct way, I think. Since this pass can occur multiple times per package and the key is the package name, but the filter result depends on the spec, only the first spec that gets here will take effect.

The result is that, in the event we have:

- `a` depends on `c==2`
- `a` is in channel-1
- `c 2.0` is only in channel-2
- `c 1.0` is in channel-1
- channel-1 has higher priority than channel-2

The simple cases:

- `conda install a`
- `conda install c==2`

will get `c 2.0` from channel-2, which is the intended change from this PR. Since it was suggested that *ranges* should not slide to lower-priority channels,

- `conda install 'c>=2'` will fail with unsatisfiable

since the exact-version condition is not triggered. Where it gets a little weird is

- `conda install a c>=2`

which fails with unsatisfiable and slightly weird:

```
Package c conflicts for:
a -> c==2
c[version='>=2']
Note that strict channel priority may have removed packages required for satisfiability.
```

This could be surprising because we have `a->c==2.0`, which picks channel-2 for c when only `a` is installed, but since `c>=2` is evaluated first and channel-priority is selected only once per package, it's picking channel-1 for c, then failing unsatisfiably on the `c==2` requirement. So these two requirements are indeed in conflict, but only because they are resulting in different channel selections, which is not communicated in the error message. I'd be okay with that, were it not the case that the exact same requirements entering the solver in a different order would be satisfiable.

I tried a bit to allow the channel-priority pass to run multiple times, which would have to allow picking channel-1 then later picking channel-2 (removing and then re-adding packages in get_reduced_index), but couldn't get it to work since reduce is expected to only decrease the acceptable packages. The result was channel-1 was picked and the solve failed with no candidate for `c==2.0` before the new channel would have been selected after adding the `c==2.0` spec.

I'm not sure it's possible to get this exactly right, or what exactly right really is.

First we start a new span trying to continue an existing trace referenced in `graph.opaque` otherwise starting a span in an effectively new trace.

Then we copy tags from `graph.opaque` into the current span.

> Is there any guarantee prefix will never already have os.sep in it?

I think this can be guaranteed because `prefix` starts off as `''` outside the while loop, and only contains parts constructed with `os.path.basename`. `os.path.basename` takes the last path element, so it is equivalent to `arg.split(os.sep)[-1]`, which will mean the result won't contain the `os.sep` character.

That said, there is something that doesn't make sense to me about this while loop. This threw me off at first, but I believe the while loop is repeatedly walking up `root` directory by directory until it hits a zip file. E.g ignoring `prefix` for a minute, the loop boils down to:

```python
while path:
  if zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
    return
  # if path = foo/bar/baz, then os.path.dirname(path) = foo/bar
  path = os.path.dirname(path)
```

Given that, I think the `prefix` is actually *backwards*. If we go into the loop with `foo.zip/bar/baz`, assuming `foo.zip` is the zip file, `prefix` will become `baz/bar`, because after the first iteration, `prefix` will be basename of the path (which is `baz`), and the second iteration joins that with basename of the path again (the original was `prefix = os.path.join(prefix, os.path.basename(path))`, so `os.path.join('baz', os.path.basename('foo.zip/bar')) = 'baz/bar'`). Am I missing something with my logic here?

This must be working because the tests pass, but I wonder if this is actually an uncaught bug? I haven't had a chance to walk through all the test cases to know if there is any test case for this, or if the caller of `_ZipIterator` somehow guarantees this loop only goes one iteration deep.

Also this loop is an infinite loop if `root` is ever an absolute path that does not contain a zip file, because `os.path.dirname('/') == '/'`, so it will never be falsy.

pandas has a function called `find_stack_level`.
https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/1d5ce5b3b42cebd4b49ef3b06b50b78e09b1d91b/pandas/util/_exceptions.py#L30-L51

```python
def find_stack_level() -> int:
    """
    Find the first place in the stack that is not inside pandas
    (tests notwithstanding).
    """

    import pandas as pd

    pkg_dir = os.path.dirname(pd.__file__)
    test_dir = os.path.join(pkg_dir, "tests")

    # https://stackoverflow.com/questions/17407119/python-inspect-stack-is-slow
    frame = inspect.currentframe()
    n = 0
    while frame:
        fname = inspect.getfile(frame)
        if fname.startswith(pkg_dir) and not fname.startswith(test_dir):
            frame = frame.f_back
            n += 1
        else:
            break
    return n
```

I don't know of many functions / mappings that are certain to produce skew-symmetric output when evaluated on some domain. The main example would be

$$ A \\mapsto \\begin{bmatrix} 0 & A^T \\\ -A & 0 \\end{bmatrix}$$

which takes any matrix $A$ to a skew-symmetric matrix with the same singular values. I'm sure there are examples where tensor products preserve skew-symmetry as well. I think it would be okay to add those features later if users actually need them.

Suppose I have sampled data which (I suspect) is from a gaussian distribution, with pdf:

```python
f(x) = exp(- (x - x0)**2 / (2 * sig**2)) / sqrt(2 * pi * sig**2)
```
The data we are confronted with are the `x`, which we suspect is from this gaussian distribution. We therefore try to find the parameters `x0` and `sig` which make this dataset the most probable. But at no point do we have access to `f(x)`. This is why we have no dependent data for a likelihood problem.

So it is not the same as first creating a histogram of our `x`, which will then give us an estimate of `f(x)` within a bin of with `dx`, which we can then fit with a least squares.

the loop inside ``with meas.run()`` that calls ``datasaver.add_results`` is implemented in such a way that in order for it to work for 0D cases, nested_setpoints must have a single empty-tuple (or emplty-sequence) value -- it semantically means "do a single 'get' of the measured parameter(s), but there are no specific setpoint values for this 'get'". The reason _make_nested_setpoints can't give that for us automatically is because ``np.vstack`` does not accept an empty list as an input (that's what happens when the list of the given sweeps is empty).

My opinion is that it's OK to let ``_make_nested_setpoints`` return ``np.array([[]])`` (empty list in a list) in case ``flat_setpoint_grids`` list inside the functions is empty. This will remove the need for the ``if sweep_instances`` clause altogether, and keeps the code readable. see here:

Ah, so we only started using this `assert` idiom recently (although Pex has always used it). Normally, this would be a bad idea. But, Pytest has magic over assert statements that make the output much nicer upon failure than unittest style assertions.

See https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/assert.html. Specifically,

```
______________________________ test_function _______________________________

    def test_function():
>       assert f() == 4
E       assert 3 == 4
E        +  where 3 = f()

test_assert1.py:6: AssertionError
```

Pytest adds statements like ` where 3 = f()` to show how each value was derived, but can only do that with `assert`.

Since `ack_ids` is a generator, iterating over a slice when building a `StreamingPullRequest` instance consumes the first `_ACK_IDS_BATCH_SIZE` items. Then in the following iteration the _next_ `_ACK_IDS_BATCH_SIZE` items are consumed.

```py
>>> from itertools import islice
>>> BATCH_SIZE = 3
>>> gen = (x for x in range(8))
>>> list(islice(gen, BATCH_SIZE))
[0, 1, 2]
>>> list(islice(gen, BATCH_SIZE))
[3, 4, 5]
>>> list(islice(gen, BATCH_SIZE))
[6, 7]
>>> list(islice(gen, BATCH_SIZE))
[]
```
Iterating over a generator has the benefit of automatically advancing the generator, sparing us the need to keep track of the slice boundaries manually.

Here is another way we can approach this:
- We do not define a `union` type in public header (we should use `union` in the core). 
   - Instead we continue to have `otNetworkKey` as before  (an array of bytes represeting the literal key)
    - And define a new `otNetworkKeyRef` which is the key ref (for PSA mode).
- For APIs that get and set the key we provide different APIs, one using `otNetworkKey` and one using `onNetworkKeyRef`.
    - We keep `otThreadSetNetworkKey(otInstance *, const otNetworkKey *)` as is
    - And add `otThreadSetNetworkKeyFromRef(otInstance *, otNetworkKeyRef aKeyRef)`.
    - Similar way for getting the key.
 - The implementation for these API is then provided only when the feature is supported (tied to related `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_{config}`) and we can document this in the public API description.


The advantages I see with this:
- It is simpler for users of API to use.
- The presence of implementation an API being tied to a `OT_CONFIG` aligns well with how it is done with many other OT APIs today (e.g, `otIp6SetSlaacEnabled`, `otDnsClinet{}`, etc).
   - The immediate benefit of this model is if an API that is not supported is used by app layer code, it causes a build/link error which is better than a run-time check.

Rather than keeping this as a private function on EventData that we then have to pylint:disable, we could move it out into a separate util function.
```python
def get_last_enqueued_event_properties(event_data):
    """Extracts the last enqueued event in from the received event delivery annotations.
    :rtype: Dict[str, Any]
    """
    if event_data.message.delivery_annotations:
        sequence_number = event_data.message.delivery_annotations.get(PROP_LAST_ENQUEUED_SEQUENCE_NUMBER, None)
        enqueued_time_stamp = event_data.message.delivery_annotations.get(PROP_LAST_ENQUEUED_TIME_UTC, None)
        if enqueued_time_stamp:
            enqueued_time_stamp = utc_from_timestamp(float(enqueued_time_stamp)/1000)
        retrieval_time_stamp = event_data.message.delivery_annotations.get(PROP_RUNTIME_INFO_RETRIEVAL_TIME_UTC, None)
        if retrieval_time_stamp:
            retrieval_time_stamp = utc_from_timestamp(float(retrieval_time_stamp)/1000)
        offset_bytes = event_data.message.delivery_annotations.get(PROP_LAST_ENQUEUED_OFFSET, None)
        offset = offset_bytes.decode('UTF-8') if offset_bytes else None
        return {
            "sequence_number": sequence_number,
            "offset": offset,
            "enqueued_time": enqueued_time_stamp,
            "retrieval_time": retrieval_time_stamp
        }
    return None
```

btw, NumPy handles it in a weird way:

```python
>>> a = np.array([[1,4],[3,1]])
>>> np.sort(a, axis=0)
array([[1, 1],
       [3, 4]])
>>> np.argsort(a, axis=0)
array([[0, 1],
       [1, 0]])
```
but
```python
>>> a[np.argsort(a, axis=0)]
array([[[1, 4],
        [3, 1]],

       [[3, 1],
        [1, 4]]])
```
The same in axis=-1:
```python
>>> np.sort(a, axis=-1)
array([[1, 4],
       [1, 3]])
>>> np.argsort(a, axis=-1)
array([[0, 1],
       [1, 0]])
>>> a[np.argsort(a, axis=-1)]
array([[[1, 4],
        [3, 1]],

       [[3, 1],
        [1, 4]]])
```

class _SetParamContext:
    """
    This class is returned by the ``set_to`` method of parameters
    Example usage:
    >>> v = dac.voltage()
    >>> with dac.voltage.set_to(-1):
        ...     # Do stuff with the DAC output set to -1 V.
        ...
    >>> assert abs(dac.voltage() - v) <= tolerance
    """
    def __init__(self, parameter: "_BaseParameter", value: ParamDataType):
        self._parameter = parameter 
        self._temp_value = value
        self._original_value = self._parameter.get_latest()

    def _value_is_changing(self) -> bool:
        return self._value != self._original_value

    def __enter__(self) -> None:
        if self._value_is_changing():
            self._parameter.set(self._value)  # type: ignore[has-type]

    def __exit__(self, typ,  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
                 value,
                 traceback) -> None:
        if self._value_is_changing:
            self._parameter.set(self._original_value)  # type: ignore[has-type]
```

dtype: float64

dtype: float64

dtype: object

attribute['BinaryValue'] = bytearray(to_bytes(value['Value']))

The change above would fix case (2) AND cover both cases (1) and (3) above:
* Case (1) - if `value['Value']` is already an encoded string, then `to_bytes` simply returns the unchanged value, and it works
* Case (2) - if `value['Value']` is an unencoded string, then `to_bytes` would convert it to an encoded string, and it works
* Case (3) - if `value['Value']` is already an encoded string, then `to_bytes` simply returns the unchanged value, and it works (this case is actually identical to (1))

Goal is to avoid ROLLBACK TO (because starting to autocommit after a ROLLBACK TO would be unexpected behavior; that syntax does not denote the end of a transaction). As far as I know, if ROLLBACK is present, the only valid SQL syntax is `ROLLBACK [WORK] TO [SAVEPOINT] identifier` (per [here](https://www.w3resource.com/mysql/mysql-transaction.php#SDT), [here](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/sql-rollback-to.html), and [here](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_savepoint.html)), and the syntax is strict enough that you can't really nest statements into a ROLLBACK. Additionally `execute` already filters against having more than one statement at a time.

The main improvement here, if any, would be checking against tokens instead of against the statement string, but even then I think such a change wouldn't really be necessary. The only other part of this syntax, per the specifications above, that can have the letters `TO` is the savepoint name -- and if we're using a savepoint name, then the keyword `TO` is required anyway. So we kind of have an invariant here that in any single SQL statement of the `ROLLBACK` type, the keyword `TO` must indicate a savepoint (even if somehow you're getting the savepoint name with a convoluted nested statement or if the savepoint name contains TO) -- which is exactly what we're trying to know. So I'm not sure it's such a fragile check, especially since it's a very readable way to get the information we need.

It is possible for the autoscaler to issue a `terminate_node` and `create_node` in quick succession within one update iteration.

It's also possible for the autoscaler to send multiple `create_node` requests in quick succession within one update, for example when spinning up workers of different groups.

In these situations, we wouldn't be surprised if the operator failed to reconcile between the scale requests. The eventual outcome would be correct (probably) -- the terminate and create requests would get spaced out between different autoscaler updates.

But the error message in the autoscaler's logs would be confusing. We should probably catch the failed test error and log something meaningful before raising the error.

Life would be easier if instead issuing of a bunch of terminate and create requests in quick succession, the autoscaler issued one scale recommendation per update consisting of what to delete and how many of each group to add.

The main difference is that in the case of SIG record `ReadSignerName()`, we still have the signature field after the name, so it updates the offset to end of the name to allow caller to read the signature field.

But in the case of PTR and SRV record the name is the last field in the record (last field we know and care about), so after reading the name we update offset to skip over the entire record.

Also I am treating the DNS records, similar to how we parse TLVs, in the sense that if there are any remaining fields/bytes in the record after the fields that we know and read, we just ignore them (this is to allow future extendibility of the record definitions). So in case of PTR/SRV, we do not assume that the offset after the name would be the same as the end of the record (we check and ensure that the name is in within the record data but when we want to skip over the record, we use the record data length directly).

iiuc, the idea is that when Angular calls our `ngAfterViewInit` lifecycle hook, it assumes we won't alter any props used in the template.  Since we do alter props used in the template, we need the explicit `detectChanges()` call.

In this PR, we are careful to only call detectChanges when necessary, but I think this adds a bit much complexity.
- L186 seem to be order-dependent, since `initializeChart` internally calls `readAndUpdateDomDimensions`, so it must come before L185.
- `updateLineChart` returns `ngStateUpdated`, but it only depends on the `viewBox` not any other state changes
- `ngStateUpdated` is not true when the viewBox changes to the 'fixed' or 'overridden' value.  Are we sure this is safe?

I think it would be a lot simpler to just call `detectChanges()` here in `ngAfterView` unconditionally, without checking whether the dim/viewbox actually changed.  If we ever updated the code to remove the `viewBox` from the template, or added new props to the template that can be updated from `ngAfterViewInit`, it would be less fragile to those changes as well.

Another way we could calculate Tukey's Five Numbers is as follows (h/t to @tylerwmarrs):

```
  if data.size < 1:
      return [np.nan] * 5
  
  x = data[~np.isnan(data)]
  x = np.sort(x)
  n = len(x)
  n4 = np.floor((n + 3) / 2) / 2
  d = np.array([1, n4, (n + 1) / 2, n + 1 - n4, n])
  i1 = (np.floor(d) - 1).astype(int)
  i2 = (np.ceil(d) - 1).astype(int)
  
  return 0.5 * (x[i1] + x[i2])
```

The above way may not actually be faster/easier given that Numpy has already optimized the percentile count, so I'm fine if we leave as-is for now.

I think this can cause some noticeable delay in some scenarios though. For example, here's a typical object restoration path

```
   node A                         node B
1. restore request  =>
2.                                      restore_object from external storage
3.                                      update the location to GCS
4. message published
5. Send a pull request => 
```
and if this path takes a long time (which can happen in some scenarios since we have two slow paths, restore_object, and GCS location update), the delay can be pretty significant since we are retrying with exponential backoff. For example, if this delays more than 700ms, the delay could be as long as a half-second (10+20+40+80+160+320+640) whereas if we handle this properly, it'd take only 10ms. 

But I agree this type of scenario is not seen in real life yet. What about this? We can but I don't see any benefit from applying of the respective call queue of right partition in the remote call here. This will be useful in case we have lazy execution (but even that is questionable, described below), but here we use `partition.apply` which runs an execution of remote calls right now.

In this row we run an early execution of the call queue in background for each `right_partition` and immediately actualize `right_partition._data`. After, during serial logic of `left_partition.apply` call for each partition we will have already finished remote calls from the call queue of the each `right_partition` with some probability.

If we will use your way we can than draining of call queue for `right_partition` will happen only after draining of call queue for `left_partition` in remote call.

Also, by providing the call queue of right partition together with `right_partition._data` in `left_partition.apply` we don't drain `right_partition.call_queue` and don't actualize the `right_partition._data`. If we will work with the right dataframe further in the code we will again run `drain_call_queue` for each partition of right dataframe. In the result we have double work of the same calls but in different places.

What benefits do you see from your suggestion? How we will resolve the issue which I described in this case?

smalltest.no:PR01:Parameters {'a'} not documented

Funny story: probl...obstacles :D

1. I don't know if the method `subclasses` is the best solution. It includes `ChainedMinimizer`, which causes an error because it's initialized without any minimizer. Maybe we have to list them in the Parametrization, like
```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize('minimizer', 
    [MINPACK, BFGS, NelderMead, ChainedMinimizer, BasinHopping, ...]
)
```
2. Such a list would help at the next point too: "Add None to the minimizer lists" sound easier as it is :D Because `subclasses` returns a set, it's difficult to combine it with `None`. With a simple Parametrization, we could add `None` (and a ChainedMinimizer instance) like
```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize('minimizer', 
    [MINPACK, BFGS, NelderMead, ChainedMinimizer, BasinHopping, ..., None, [BFGS, NelderMead]]
)
```
3. Another obstacle we have to overcome is `stdev`. Because we use different minimizers, we have different stdevs. We could overcome it by using `pytest.approx(value, abs=1e-3`) for example. Easy...
4. A major obstacle is the deviating values *a* and *b* with the minimizers `COBYLA`, `TrustConstr`. 
5. DifferentialEvolution makes problems together with scipy: "ValueError: bounds should be a sequence containing real valued (min, max) pairs for each value in x"...Probably because of the model (?)
6. What should we do with our next "problem child": `MINPACK` and `VectorLeastSquares`? Own test, but with `_run_test` in my opinion :D

Initially, I had it the way you described, where claiming removed a step from `pending_steps`.  But the math / locking logic got too complicated across tables, so now there is a row in `pending_steps` for all in flight steps including ones that are currently occupying a slot.  This means that every executing step should occupy a row in the slots table and the pending steps table.

This is the state progression for steps that want to claim a slot:
1.  Step is waiting for a slot to open up (unassigned).  This state is skipped if there are more open slots than pending steps (e.g. the pending step is assigned immediately on write).
2.  A slot has opened up and the highest priority step is tapped to claim it but has not yet claimed it (assigned).
3.  The assigned step has woken up from sleep in the executor and has claimed an open slot.
4. The step has completed so the slot row is cleared and the pending step row is removed.

That's the gist of the test - if the lock is held by the batch commit thread while the batch is being committed, i.e. when the code is waiting for `self._client.api.publish()` to return, the operation will take more than one second and the test will fail.

If the lock is released right after the state transition to `IN_PROGRESS`, i.e. before the call to `self._client.api.publish()`, the latter will not block other threads that might (indirectly) call `batch.publish()` in the meantime and try to acquire the state lock.

Hmm... I'm not really following the `files` and `cwd` bit fully, but:

I think in pants lingo, any file is considered a `resource` to the `package.json`: All files that will be included in a package .tar are expected to be at or below the directory containing the `package.json`, when "packaging" (npm pack). But the pipeline is scriptable, and the scripts themselves have no limitations to where they can copy files from.

The `package.json` has fields that references where files to include in packages are found, but either silently ignores `../` or errors, depending on package manager. The default is to implicitly include all files next to and below the `package.json`, with a `.gitignore`-esque blocklist.

In source code, files are either loaded via `import "./path/to/file.json"` or via file reading api:s similar to pythons `open` (of course...). Same thing here is that the import cannot "escape" the `package.json` directory, whilst `open` could.

So I guess if a `package_json` happens to have a dependency on a `resource` that isn't at or below it's containing directory, pants could error or warn?

I think `file` is the same, strictly speaking? But that is disregarding scriptability. Users could have scripts in `package.json` to e.g copy files from outside into the `package.json` dir that executes as part of `npm pack`. Pants has facilities for this as well though in `relocated_files`, so maybe encouraging using that is better? :thinking:

Yes that's correct and intentional; we don't need to do anything for the `LegacyPlugin` branch.

`LegacyPlugin` is a wrapper for plugins written for our old plugin system. This includes our old pillow plugin, which is the original reason for having `pilmode`. It will do the right thing without intervention from our side.

Trivia: The reason we have `pilmode` in the first place is that the old plugin system uses `mode` as a kwarg to decide if it should read or write. `mode` is also used by pillow to indicate the colorspace, and also by other backends to indicate various "modes". Obviously this is not ideal. Our new plugin uses `io_mode` to decide reading or writing, which frees up the name `mode` and allows plugins to use the name as a kwarg. Hence, our new pillow plugin can use `mode` to indicate the colorspace to use, which is what you would expect from pillow (since this is how it uses `mode`).

Looking at this for the first time today, so I may be missing something, but if I'm reading this correctly, on the client side, we're coupling putting a message on the queue with a TCP network request to send a Thrift (or possibly HTTP in the future) message to the sidecar. Unless I"m missing something, this will force the client to wait for an acknowledgment from the server, both at TCP level as well as the l7 protocol level. This is a significant change in behavior from how the POSIX queue is going to behave, where the messages are, outside of exceptional cases, going to be always acknowledged by the queue -- now we're dependent on server behavior. If the server degrades in any way, the service emitting events is going to see latency impact, or worse.

Running against a local sidecar will hide latency issues in the common case, but if the sidecar server wedges, or anything like that, we're going to be in trouble.

If we need delivery guarantees here that are leading us to use TCP and protocols that provide message acknowledgement, I think we'll need to do some client side buffering of these events for this so we can decouple the main baseplate app from the behavior of the server. This is going to increase the memory footprint of every baseplate application though because we have to account for that size of that message buffer now, but that should be an acceptable tradeoff.

Again, this is just based on my initial reading here, so I might be wrong.

So the question is whether the class `FretBoard` could also be used to describe the string instrument linked to a whole tablature *staff*. Looking at the code of `FretBoard`, it seems to me that it is too related to *positions* (see for examples methods `getFretNoteByString` which does not make sense in the context of a whole tablature staff). So I'm starting to think that we might not want to use `Fretboard` here (I'm also wondering if to avoid futur similar confusion, we could consider renaming the class `FretBoard` in something like `FretBoardPosition`, but it would require changing the name of many related classes / methods with similarly ambiguous names in `tablature.py` like `FretNote`, etc.).

On the other hand, I agree that the tuning of the string instrument is not supposed to affect the layout of the staff so using `StaffLayout` might not seem justified here. In the update mode, the parameter value is not obtained from `mc` because we just need to use the value provided by the user to overwrite the value in `mc`.

But in the create mode, `mc` is completely blank, and we want to assemble the user's input into a complete `mc`. Considering that our parameters have a certain dependency or mutual exclusion relationship, or the parameters will be normalized before putting into `mc`, there are even some parameters that do not need user to provide explicitly, but can be dynamically completed by cli. In order to ensure the consistency of the state and make the value of the parameter more clearly determined, we created the following principle in the create mode, that is, if a parameter has been placed in the `mc` object, then when trying to get the parameter value again, (e.g. cross-validation with other parameters) the value in `mc` shall prevail.

Going back to the example in the windows profile, the current windows related parameters will not be used after being set into `mc`, but in order to keep the form consistent with other parameters, we keep such acquisition method in the create mode.

This word probably sprang as a differentiator from the fact you could register "targets", "objects", and CAOFs in Pants V1. A CAOF has the constructor you use here and ~must use the context to do anything useful.

An object was simply a registered alias for a plain old python type that got exposed in BUILD files for direct construction. An example was:
```python
python_library(
  ...
  provides=setup_py(...)
)
```
There any `python_library` could gain the ability to define what today is a separate `python_distribution` target. To do so you used the `setup_py` object.

```pycon
>>> # get these from self.get_commit_message(self.commit_sha1).splitlines()
>>> with open('msg.txt') as f:
...   lines = [line.strip() for line in f]  # remove trailing whitespace/newlines
... 
>>> # get these from `git interpret-trailers --only-trailers`
>>> trailers = ['Co-authored-by: Co-Author Name <co@author.email>',
...             'Co-authored-by: Author Name <author@name.email>']
>>> # remove trailers from the full message
>>> for trailer in trailers:
...   lines.remove(trailer)
... 
>>> msg = '\n'.join(lines).strip()  # this is the msg without trailers
>>> trailers.append('Co-authored-by: Another Author')
>>> metadata = '\n'.join(trailers)  # these are the updated trailers
>>> print(f"""[3.10] {msg}
... (cherry-picked from somewhere)
... 
... {metadata}""")  # this is the final message
[3.10] gh-12345: fix some broken stuff

Some stuff was broken and now
it's fixed.
(cherry-picked from somewhere)

Co-authored-by: Co-Author Name <co@author.email>
Co-authored-by: Author Name <author@name.email>
Co-authored-by: Another Author
```
That is, if you take the full commit and remove the trailer lines you obtained with `git interpret-trailers --only-trailers` you'll be left with the message only. `for` is the slowest.

![speed](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4003950/116071641-b4ab7800-a6c0-11eb-967f-2cd6d0ceb686.png)

```py
import time

# Compare runtime of three methods
list_sizes = [i * 400000 for i in range(30)]
runtimes_1 = []  # Method 1: + Operator
runtimes_2 = []  # Method 2: += Operator
runtimes_3 = []  # Method 3: extend()
runtimes_4 = []  # Method 3: for
for size in list_sizes:
    to_add = list(range(size))

    # Get time stamps
    time_0 = time.time()
    lst = [1]
    lst = lst + to_add
    time_1 = time.time()
    lst = [1]
    lst += to_add
    time_2 = time.time()
    lst = [1]
    lst.extend(to_add)
    time_3 = time.time()
    lst = [1]
    for i in to_add:
        lst.append(i)
    time_4 = time.time()
    # Calculate runtimes
    runtimes_1.append((size, time_1 - time_0))
    runtimes_2.append((size, time_2 - time_1))
    runtimes_3.append((size, time_3 - time_2))
    runtimes_4.append((size, time_4 - time_3))
# Plot everything
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

runtimes_1 = np.array(runtimes_1)
runtimes_2 = np.array(runtimes_2)
runtimes_3 = np.array(runtimes_3)
runtimes_4 = np.array(runtimes_4)
print(runtimes_1)
print(runtimes_2)
print(runtimes_3)
print(runtimes_4)
plt.plot(runtimes_1[:, 0], runtimes_1[:, 1], label='Method 1: +')
plt.plot(runtimes_2[:, 0], runtimes_2[:, 1], label='Method 2: +=')
plt.plot(runtimes_3[:, 0], runtimes_3[:, 1], label='Method 3: extend()')
plt.plot(runtimes_4[:, 0], runtimes_4[:, 1], label='Method 4: for')
plt.xlabel('list size')
plt.ylabel('runtime (seconds)')
plt.legend()
plt.savefig('speed.png', dpi=400)
